dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems at 118 in the city of [CITY].
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with medical rescue. What made you call 911?
I have severe shortness of breath that started two hours ago while I was cooking.
Can you describe your breathing difficulty in more detail?
I can't take deep breaths. My inhaler isn't working anymore.
Ma'am, I need to check your responsiveness. What day is it?
Tuesday, I think.
Can you state your full name for me?
Elena, E-lena.
Where are you right now?
In my apartment in [CITY].
Can you explain what happened before you called 911?
I was making dinner when I started to feel out of breath and it kept getting worse.
Your airway is patent and you appear to be maintaining it well.
Your breathing is very labored. I'm going to listen to your lungs right now.
Your respirations are extremely rapid, about 40 per minute.
SpO2 is currently 85%. We will have you start a non-rebreather mask.
Pulse is very fast, 140 per minute, and it's strong.
We're unable to obtain blood pressure at this time.
Blood glucose is not displaying, but clinically not a concern at this time.
The EKG is flat; we will record a 12-lead once assessments are complete.

Are you experiencing any other physical symptoms? 23. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs_and_symptoms; Patient: No chest pain, just difficulty breathing and I feel like I can't catch my breath.
What medications do you take on a regular basis? 25. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; Patient: My inhaler, which is albuterol.
Do you have a medical history of breathing-related conditions?
Yes, I have asthma and COPD, and sarcoidosis.
Do you currently have a history of any heart conditions or intubation?
I haven't had any heart problems or been intubated before.
Let's talk about where and what happened when your symptoms first started.
I was cooking earlier when it all began, and it did not go away after using the inhaler.
Your skin is dry, warm, and has a normal color.
You appear to have elevated neck veins, or jugular venous distension.
While breathing, do you notice yourself using any muscles other than your chest?
I can definitely feel my shoulders and neck lifting with each breath.
I'm going to place a cardiac monitor so we can observe your heart rhythm.
EKG remains flat; we will record a 4-lead now for baseline monitoring.
SpO2 improved to 99% after we started the CPAP.
You will be placed on CPAP to improve your breathing more effectively.
A nebulizer treatment using Albuterol and Atrovent is now being set up.
Please take the inhalation slowly and deeply during the nebulizer treatment.
Your respiration rate is now 31 per minute, less labored but still significantly elevated.
Heart rate is 146 per minute, still very high.
Blood pressure is 131 over 82 now, showing some improvement.
The EKG now shows a sinus rhythm, which is better.
We will give you an intramuscular injection of epinephrine for the breathing difficulty.
We'll place an IV in your left forearm for future medication administration.
Next, you'll be receiving dexamethasone intravenously to help reduce airway swelling.
We are now recording a second 12-lead EKG for comparison.
The 12-lead shows evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy.
There's also some left atrial enlargement noted on this reading.
We'll do a second nebulizer treatment, this time with just albuterol.
Magnesium will be given next via IV pump to further improve your airway opening.
We are following the respiratory distress protocol due to your physical findings and responses.
Heart rate remains elevated at 142 per minute.
Your breathing is now at 46 per minute and remains very labored.
EKG transitioned to sinus tachycardia.
Ma'am, are you aware of a preferred hospital for care?
I prefer to go to [COMPANY_NAME].
We will assist you to stand and then safely move you onto the stretcher.
The stretcher is now locked and ready for transfer.
All emergency systems are confirmed and transport is authorized.
The ambulance is now en route; we're on our way.
Trauma nurse, we have a 41-year-old female in respiratory distress with preserved mental status.
Patient is 15/15 on GCS: E4, V5, M6 and unremarkable for pain.
The 12-lead suggests possible atrial flutter and T wave irregularities.
You're still exhibiting significant accessory muscle use with breathing.
Elevated respiratory effort with noticeable retractions and shoulder raising is observed.
The initial EKG was flat due to low SpO2 but normalized after CPAP was applied.
Skin is warm, dry, and shows no abnormal coloring.
No pain noted during respiration or history of chest discomfort or heart failure.

Blood glucose is not detectable, but clinical examination reveals no signs of altered level of consciousness. 73. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs_and_symptoms; Partner: No chest discomfort or sweating is reported.
JVD is still present, but no neck stiffness or palpable masses are found.
Have you eaten or drunk anything recently?
Yes, I had soup around 11 PM last night.
The patient's temperature was 99°F as checked using the tympanic method.
Patient is secured using a five-point restraint system for transport.
Two fire department personnel are on board to assist during transit.
The emergency department has been notified about our expected arrival.
Comfortable position secured on the stretcher with restraint device engaged.
All handoff procedures are complete.
Patient is fully secured and ready for safe transfer.
Patient continues to breathe without reported pain.
Sheet transfer technique is not being used at this time.
The patient is now on the hospital bed and all safety rails are raised.
Care is now being transferred to the ED nurse with full documentation.
All administered medications and interventions are on record and communicated.
The patient remains in a comfortable and safe position for transport.
EMS crew is leaving the scene with full continuity of care.
The transport destination remains consistent and communicated to the facility.
On a 0 to 10 scale, how bad is it right now?
I would say 8 at the moment; it's still very difficult to breathe.
Does your difficulty radiate to any other areas of the body?
Sometimes I feel it in my back, but not in the arms or chest.
EKG remains stable with no dangerous rhythms during transport.
CPAP is maintained at 5 cm H2O and breath sounds have improved.
A second nebulizer dose was given in response to ongoing shortness of breath.
Second albuterol only nebulizer given per protocol, with good clinical response.
Ambulance is on the hospital grounds, and the facility is aware.
Firefighters are present to assist with transition to the hospital bed.
Care is being handed over to the ED personnel.
Complete care transfer includes ECG strips, treatment plan, and real-time vitals.
The patient is stable, alert, and is being taken into ED care.
Firefighters remain on-scene to assist during transfer to the ED bed.
All EMS equipment and patient narrative is handed over to receiving staff.
The patient is being monitored on a continuous cardiac monitor in the ED.
EMS crew will remain onboard until ED notification of full transfer of care.
EMS is leaving after a full report to the ED team.
The patient is admitted in a stable condition and protocol followed.
All communication with the receiving provider is complete with documentation sign-off.
Transfer and scene vacated per protocol.
All transport operations are completed and patient is appropriately placed in care.
